reasoning: Vps33a encodes a core subunit of the HOPS and CORVET tethering complexes that mediate endosome‑lysosome and vacuolar fusion. Its primary role is in intracellular membrane trafficking and organelle biogenesis rather than in transcriptional regulation. Ksr1 (Kinase suppressor of Ras 1) functions as a scaffold for the Ras‑Raf‑MEK‑ERK MAPK cascade and its expression is mainly controlled by signaling pathways and transcription factors that respond to growth factor cues. There is no established mechanistic link whereby loss of a vesicle‑tethering factor such as Vps33a would directly alter Ksr1 transcription; any effect would be indirect and likely modest, arising only from secondary cellular stress. Consequently, CRISPRi‑mediated knockdown of Vps33a is not expected to cause a consistent up‑ or down‑regulation of Ksr1 expression.
